title: Systematic Fine-Tuning of MACE Interatomic Potentials for Catalysis

claims.text: Table 2 'All Reactions' column total is reported as 1569, but the sum of its per-dataset entries (170 + 512 + 840 + 217; BMA row is '-') equals 1739.
claims.predicate: Sum the 'All Reactions' column entries across the five dataset rows and compare to the reported Total cell.

evidence.quote: C2+ oxygenates ... 170 ; NH3 synthesis ... 512 ; C2H6 dehydrogenation ... 840 ; O, OH adsorption ... 217 ; Total ... 1569

verification.verifier_needed: table-total / column-sum verifier (off-the-shelf 'table totals' check)
verification.rationale: 170+512+840+217 = 1739 != 1569 reported. A genuine column-total mismatch IF the column is meant to sum (BMA cell is '-'). Confidence held below 0.9 because the table semantics of 'All Reactions' (which counts enumerations across terminations/compositions) may not be intended to be additive across rows, and the BMA '-' is ambiguous; an off-the-shelf table-total verifier would flag it for human disambiguation.

claims.text: The abstract states the headline FT MLIP 'fine-tuned to 49,860 configurations'; this matches the FT-All NFT-TS in Table 5 and the Table 2 grand total.
claims.quote: a large MLIP fine-tuned to 49,860 configurations shows the best performance across both metallic and metal-oxide catalysts
claims.predicate: Cross-check the abstract's 49,860 against FT-All NFT-TS (Table 5) and the Table 2 Configs total.

evidence.quote: fine-tuned to 49,860 configurations
FT-All 49,860

verification.verifier_needed: prose-vs-table consistency check on a single quantity
verification.rationale: Abstract 49,860 == Table 5 FT-All NFT-TS == Table 2 Configs total. Internally consistent across three locations.

claims.text: The abstract claims an MLIP fine-tuned on metallic catalysts achieves an MAE of 0.30 eV for OER on iridium oxide polymorphs, which is 0.08 eV lower than out-of-box MACE-MH-1 and 0.14 eV lower than the best-performing FS MLIP.
claims.quote: an MLIP fine-tuned on metallic catalysts achieves an MAE of 0.30 eV for OER on iridium oxide polymorphs, which is 0.08 eV lower than out-of-box MACE-MH-1 and 0.14 eV lower than the best performing FS MLIP
claims.predicate: Bind the abstract's 0.30 / 0.08 / 0.14 eV to the §4.2.2 OER body values (FT-All 0.278, FT-MD(MO) 0.313, MACE-MH-1 0.384, FS-All 0.438) and recompute the differences.

evidence.quote: MAE of 0.30 eV for OER on iridium oxide polymorphs, which is 0.08 eV lower than out-of-box MACE-MH-1 and 0.14 eV lower than the best performing FS MLIP

The total MAEs for the 96 reaction for FT-MD(MO) and FT-All models are 0.313 and 0.278 eV, respectively ... Using the MACE-MH-1 model (no fine-tuning) ... has an overall MAE of 0.384 eV ... the FS-All model has an MAE of 0.438 eV

verification.verifier_needed: prose-vs-prose/figure cross-consistency + percent/delta verifier (binding which body number the abstract's 0.30 refers to)
verification.rationale: 0.384 - 0.30 = 0.084 (~0.08) and 0.438 - 0.30 = 0.138 (~0.14), so the abstract's two DELTAS are internally consistent with using 0.30 as the reference. BUT 0.30 itself matches no single reported body value: FT-All OER = 0.278 and FT-MD(MO) = 0.313; the abstract elsewhere headlines FT-All OER = 0.278 (Conclusion) and Table 5 FT-All Er(MO) = 0.277. The 0.30 appears to be a loose round/representative figure rather than a transcribed result. Also note the abstract says 'fine-tuned on metallic catalysts', which best fits FT-MD(M) or FT-BMA, but the OER numbers quoted are for FT-All / FT-MD(MO) (which include metal-oxide data) — a possible model/scope mismatch. Flag for human: which model and which number does '0.30 eV' denote?

claims.text: FS models with 5-10% perturbed (high-energy) configurations from MD or CE can reduce the error by over 2x compared to models trained only on relaxation trajectories.
claims.quote: FS models with 5 % to 10 % of perturbed (high-energy) configurations from MD or contour exploring (CE), can reduce the error by over 2x compared to models trained only on relaxation trajectories
claims.predicate: Bind 'reduce error by over 2x' to Table 4 (FS-BMA vs FS-All / FS-BMA+MD) and recompute the ratio; also check the 5-10% perturbed-fraction figure against config counts.

verification.verifier_needed: percent/delta (ratio) verifier with claim<->table binding
verification.rationale: Er(M): 0.659/0.279 = 2.36x; Ea(M): 0.398/0.175 = 2.27x; both exceed 2x, consistent with 'over 2x'. Confidence < 0.9 because 'the error' is unspecified (Er or Ea?) and the 5-10% perturbed fraction (2,209 CE + ~7,659 MD on ~49,860) needs its own arithmetic binding; the ratio claim itself recomputes as supported.

claims.text: An MLIP fine-tuned to O and OH adsorption on metal oxides gives an Ea MAE of 0.19 eV for the out-of-distribution CO2RR reaction on Cu.
claims.quote: an MLIP fine-tuned to O and OH adsorption on metal oxides gives an Ea MAE of 0.19 eV for out-of-distribution CO2 RR reaction on Cu
claims.predicate: Bind 0.19 eV to the FT-MD(MO) Ea value. Table 5 lists FT-MD(MO) Ea(M) = 0.184 eV; SI Table 6 lists FT-MD(MO) Ea = 0.149 eV for the 23 Ref.[62] reactions. Determine which the abstract's 0.19 refers to.

verification.verifier_needed: prose-vs-table binding across abstract and two tables (which Ea aggregate is 0.19?)
verification.rationale: Abstract 0.19 eV does not exactly match Table 5 FT-MD(MO) Ea(M) = 0.184 (rounds to 0.18) nor SI Table 6 = 0.149. 0.184 rounds to 0.18, not 0.19, so the abstract appears to round up or refer to a Cu-specific subset not separately tabulated. Cannot bind to a unique evidence number, so inconclusive rather than suspect; the abstract's parenthetical 'equivalent to an FS model trained to in-distribution carbon bond-breaking on Cu' is the comparative claim, separately checkable only if that FS Cu Ea is tabulated (it is not given explicitly).

claims.text: The FT-All model fine-tuned to 49,860 configurations was used to screen a large left-out set of 90,781 bimetallic alloy structures, achieving an MAE of 0.15 eV for Er.
claims.quote: which was used to screen a large-left out, 90,781, of bimetallic alloys, achieving an MAE of 0.15 eV for Er
claims.predicate: Check scope: the 0.15 eV MAE is computed on a 2,115-structure DFT-validated subset, not on all 90,781 screened structures. Verify the abstract does not overstate the support set for the 0.15 eV figure.

evidence.quote: This procedure produced 90,781 total initialized structures, of which 90,181 converged ... we selected 2,115 structures ... For these 2,115 configurations, the FT-All model adsorption energy MAE is 0.15 eV

verification.verifier_needed: scope/claim<->evidence support verifier (does the error metric's support set match the screened set?)
verification.rationale: The 0.15 eV is genuinely measured on a 2,115-structure DFT subset of the 90,781 screened, and the body is explicit about this. The abstract phrasing ('screen ... 90,781 ... achieving an MAE of 0.15 eV') is defensible because the MAE characterizes the screening accuracy via a representative sample; mild over-compression but not a misrepresentation. Marked supported with moderate confidence; a strict scope verifier might downgrade to 'suspect' for conflating screened-N with validated-N.

claims.text: FS-All achieves an MAE of 0.175 eV for Ea(M) predictions, about half of the error of the FS-BMA+MD model.
claims.quote: This model achieves an MAE of 0.175 eV for Ea (M) predictions, about half of the error calculated using the FS-BMA+MD model
claims.predicate: Recompute 0.175 / FS-BMA+MD Ea(M) and check it is ~0.5.

verification.verifier_needed: percent/ratio verifier with prose<->table binding
verification.rationale: 0.175 / 0.398 = 0.44, i.e. 'about half' (slightly less than half). Consistent with the prose and the table values; the table Ea(M) values for FS-BMA+MD (0.398) and FS-All (0.175) both transcribe correctly into this claim.

claims.text: FS-All is within 0.05 eV of the UMA and MACE-MH-1 models' Ea(M) MAE.
claims.quote: This model achieves an MAE of 0.175 eV for Ea (M) predictions ... within 0.05 eV of the UMA and MACE-MH-1 models' MAE.
claims.predicate: Compare FS-All Ea(M) 0.175 against UMA(OC20) 0.131 and MACE-MH-1(OC20) 0.176 from Table 4; check all differences <= 0.05 eV.

evidence.quote: FS-All 0.175 ; UMA (OC20) 0.131 ; UMA (OMAT) 0.453 ; MACE-MP0 0.493 ; MACE-MH-1 (OC20) 0.176 ; MACE-MH-1 (OMAT) 0.220

verification.verifier_needed: claim<->table threshold/delta verifier
verification.rationale: 0.175 vs UMA(OC20) 0.131 = 0.044 (<=0.05, OK) and vs MACE-MH-1(OC20) 0.176 = 0.001 (OK). HOWEVER UMA(OMAT)=0.453 and MACE-MH-1(OMAT)=0.220 give differences 0.278 and 0.045; the latter is borderline and the OMAT variants differ markedly. The claim 'within 0.05 eV of UMA and MACE-MH-1' holds only for the OC20 variants (and MH-1 OMAT at 0.045) but NOT for UMA(OMAT). Whether the claim implicitly means the best/OC20 variant is the binding ambiguity; flag for human. Numbers themselves transcribe correctly.

verification.rationale: 0.310 < 0.403 (UMA OC20), < 0.558 (UMA OMAT), < 0.578 (MH-1 OC20), < 0.478 (MH-1 OMAT). FS-BMA+MD Er(M) is lower than every listed UMA/MACE-MH-1 Er(M) value. Claim recomputes as supported directly from Table 4.

claims.predicate: Compare FT-BMA (Table 5: Er(M) 0.238, Er(MO) 0.334, Ea(M) 0.145) against FS-All (Table 4: Er(M) 0.279, Er(MO) 0.398, Ea(M) 0.175) metric-by-metric.

verification.verifier_needed: multi-metric claim<->table comparison verifier
verification.rationale: FT-BMA beats FS-All on all three: Er(M) 0.238<0.279, Er(MO) 0.334<0.398, Ea(M) 0.145<0.175. 'Better than' holds across the board. Note also it achieves this with NFT-TS=39,983 vs FS-All's 49,860, supporting the efficiency framing.

claims.text: With no metal-oxide in the FT-BMA training set, the Er(MO) MAE is 0.334 eV, about 0.06 eV lower than a model that had about 2,692 metal-oxide configurations in the training set (FT-MD(MO), Er(MO)=0.306).
claims.quote: with no metal oxide in the training set, the Er (MO) MAE is 0.334 eV, about 0.06 eV lower than a model that had about 2,692 metal-oxides configurations in the training set
claims.predicate: Bind 'a model with ~2,692 metal-oxide configs' to FT-MD(MO) (Er(MO)=0.306) and recompute 0.334 - 0.306.

verification.verifier_needed: percent/delta verifier with prose<->table binding
verification.rationale: 0.334 - 0.306 = 0.028 eV, which rounds to ~0.03, NOT ~0.06 as stated, IF the comparison model is FT-MD(MO) (the model with 2,692 MO configs, Er(MO)=0.306). The direction is also notable: FT-BMA (0.334) is HIGHER, not lower, than FT-MD(MO) (0.306) on Er(MO), yet the prose says 0.334 is '0.06 eV lower'. This appears internally inconsistent. Possible alternative binding: FT-MD(M) Er(MO)=0.428 gives 0.428-0.334=0.094 (~0.09); FT-BMA+MD(M) Er(MO)=0.348 gives 0.014. None yields 0.06 with the correct sign. Flag as suspect / prose-table mismatch; confidence held at 0.5 because the intended comparison model is ambiguous (the '2,692 metal-oxides configurations' descriptor most directly matches FT-MD(MO), against which the sign is wrong).

claims.text: FT-MD(M) performs worse than FT-BMA with a 0.15 eV increase in Er(M) MAE.
claims.quote: The FT-MD(M) model performs worse than the FT-BMA model with a 0.15 eV increase in Er (M) MAE.
claims.predicate: Recompute FT-MD(M) Er(M) - FT-BMA Er(M) = 0.376 - 0.238.

verification.verifier_needed: percent/delta verifier with prose<->table binding
verification.rationale: 0.376 - 0.238 = 0.138 eV, which the authors round to '0.15 eV increase'. 0.138 rounds to 0.14 conventionally; the paper's 0.15 is a slight over-round but defensible as 'a ~0.15 eV increase'. Direction (FT-MD(M) worse) is correct. Marginally suspect on the rounding (0.138 -> 0.15) but within reasonable reporting tolerance.

claims.text: After fine-tuning on all datasets (FT-All), the Er(MO) MAE decreases by 0.07 eV relative to the previous FT models.
claims.quote: after fine-tuning on all of our datasets (FT-All), the Er (M) and Ea (M) MAE are unchanged, the Er (MO) MAE decreases by 0.07 eV.

verification.rationale: Relative to FT-BMA+MD(M): Er(MO) 0.348 -> 0.277 = 0.071 decrease (~0.07, supported). Er(M) 0.245 -> 0.247 (essentially unchanged, +0.002) and Ea(M) 0.152 -> 0.144 (-0.008, ~unchanged) are consistent with 'unchanged' to two decimals. Binding to FT-BMA+MD(M) as 'the previous FT model' is the natural reading and yields exactly 0.07.

claims.text: For the CHCOH* pathway of the CO2RR (7 reactions on Cu(001)), MACE-MH-1(OMAT) MAE=1.011 eV, FS-BMA=0.455 eV, FS-All=0.251 eV, and FT-All=0.141 eV is the best.
claims.quote: MACE-MH-1 (OMAT) performs the worst with an MAE of 1.011 eV. The FS-BMA model exhibits an error ... (overall MAE of 0.455 eV) ... reduced to 0.251 eV by the FS-All model ... FT-All model ... best performing model with an MAE of 0.141 eV across these 7 reactions
claims.predicate: Cross-check the four MAE values against Fig. 3a; verify the ordering claim (FT-All lowest, MACE-MH-1 highest) is consistent with the recomputable per-step errors plotted.

evidence.quote: MAE of 1.011 eV ... overall MAE of 0.455 eV ... reduced to 0.251 eV ... MAE of 0.141 eV across these 7 reactions

verification.verifier_needed: figure-reading verifier: read per-step MLIP-vs-DFT energies off Fig 3a and recompute the 4 MAEs over the 7-step pathway
verification.rationale: The four MAEs are mutually ordered as claimed (1.011 > 0.455 > 0.251 > 0.141) and the conclusion restates FT-All=0.141, so internal consistency holds. A true verdict requires reading the plotted solid/dashed line values off Fig 3a and recomputing MAE over the 7 steps; without the underlying per-step numbers (only the figure and Zenodo data) the recompute is not bindable from the text alone. Data IS on Zenodo (10.5281/zenodo.20043317), so this is T6-reproducible in principle. Inconclusive pending figure extraction or data rerun.

Note a side-consistency check: FS-All total = 49,860 (Table 2); 49,860 - 2,692 (MO) = 47,168 metallic, exactly matching the stated 47,168 metallic surfaces — that subtraction IS deterministically confirmable (49,860-2,692=47,168). The per-step error magnitudes require figure extraction.

The arithmetic 2692+47168=49860 holds; the source-count transcription is off by 1 across locations. Low-severity suspect.

claims.text: On the binary-alloy screening test set, accuracy is lowest for the (532) facet with only 58% of predictions within +/-0.2 eV, while (311) has 77% and all other terminations 65-75%.
claims.quote: For the (311) surface termination, 77% of configurations are within the target threshold of +-0.2 eV. The accuracy is lowest for the (532) facet ... with only 58% of predictions within the error threshold. Nevertheless for all other surface terminations, 65% to 75% of the tested configurations lie within the +-0.2 eV tolerance.
claims.predicate: Read the per-facet bee-swarm distributions in Fig 6a, count points within the +/-0.2 eV dashed band, and recompute the percentages (58%, 77%, 65-75%).

evidence.quote: Bee-swarm plots ... grouped by (a) surface termination ... The dashed red lines indicates the 0.2 eV threshold

verification.verifier_needed: figure-reading verifier (count within-band fraction per facet) OR data rerun on the 2,115-structure test set
verification.rationale: Percentages are computable from the bee-swarm point clouds in Fig 6a or directly from the 2,115-structure errors on Zenodo, but not from text. Internally, '(532) lowest at 58%' and '(311)=77%' are consistent with 'all other terminations 65-75%' only if 311 is treated as an exception above the band (77% > 75%) — a slight phrasing tension worth noting. T6/figure check needed to settle the exact counts.

The narrative point ('error does not strongly correlate with atomic-radii mismatch', since CuIr at 73% beats AgNi at 68% despite ordering) is a T4 claim resting on these numbers; the numbers themselves need figure/data extraction.

claims.text: The conclusion states 81% of a randomly selected subset of reactions have less than +/-0.2 eV error with respect to DFT in the binary-alloy screening.
claims.quote: we applied the FT-All model to screen adsorption energy predictions ... with 81% of randomly selected subset of reactions having less than +-0.2 eV error with respect to the DFT values.
claims.predicate: Recompute the overall fraction of the 2,115-structure test set within +/-0.2 eV and check it equals 81%; cross-check against the per-facet (58-77%) and per-composition (46-73%) breakdowns for aggregate consistency.

evidence.quote: 81% of randomly selected subset of reactions having less than +-0.2 eV error
per-facet 58% to 77%; per-composition 46% to 73%

verification.verifier_needed: aggregate-vs-subgroup consistency verifier (does an 81% overall reconcile with subgroup fractions of 46-77%?) + data rerun
verification.rationale: An overall 81%-within-band is numerically possible alongside subgroup fractions of 46-77% only if the subgroups shown in Fig 6 are unrepresentative or unequally weighted, since 81% exceeds every individual per-facet (max 77%) and per-composition (max 73%) value reported. A weighted average of the displayed subgroups would generally fall below 81%, so the 81% headline sits in tension with the breakdowns. This is checkable by recomputing the within-band fraction over the full 2,115-structure set (Zenodo). Flag as suspect pending that recompute; the apparent inconsistency may be explained by the random subset differing from the grouped subsets shown.

claims.text: The binary-alloy screening space is built from 8 FCC metals in 3 mixing ratios (100/0%, 25/75%, 50/50%), 6 surface terminations, and 33 adsorbates, yielding 56 total surface compositions.
claims.quote: binary metal surfaces selected from 8 common FCC metal alloys in 3 different mixing ratios ... and the (001), (111), (211), (311), (511), and (532) facets ... we placed 33 different adsorbates ... seven surface compositions were selected out of the 56 total surface compositions
claims.predicate: Check the combinatorics: number of unordered metal pairs from 8 elements = C(8,2)=28; with 2 mixed ratios (25/75, 50/50, where 50/50 is symmetric and 25/75 gives 2 orderings) plus 8 pure (100/0) compositions; reconcile to the stated 56 total surface compositions and 6 facets and 33 adsorbates.

evidence.quote: 8 common FCC metal alloys ... 3 different mixing ratios ... 6 ... facets ... 33 different adsorbates ... 56 total surface compositions

verification.verifier_needed: combinatorial-consistency verifier (reconcile element/ratio counts to 56 compositions and 90,781 structures)
verification.rationale: 56 surface compositions is plausibly C(8,2)=28 pairs x 2 mixed ratios (25/75 and 50/50) = 56, which is internally consistent if pure metals are counted separately or excluded; the exact accounting (whether 100/0 pure metals add 8, and whether 25/75 is directionally distinct) is not fully specified, so the 56 cannot be uniquely re-derived from text. The 33 adsorbates are enumerated in SI Table 7 (countable). The jump from compositions x facets x adsorbates to 90,781 depends on per-surface unique-site counts (SI S8), not deterministically reconstructable. Inconclusive on the full combinatorics; the 33-adsorbate count is independently checkable against SI Table 7.

claims.text: SI Table 7 lists 33 adsorbates targeted in the screening task.
claims.quote: The list of 33 adsorbates that were targeted in the screening task.
claims.predicate: Count the distinct adsorbate species enumerated in SI Table 7 and check it equals 33.

evidence.quote: C, CCH, CCH2, CCH3, CCHO, CCHOH, CCO, CHCH, CH2, CH2O, CH3, CH4, CHCH2, CHCHO, CHCO, CHO, CHOCHO, CHOH, COCH2O, COCH3, COCHO, COH, COHCHO, COHCOH, NH2, NH3, NONH, OCHCH2, OHCH3, OOH, CHCOH, CH2CO, COCOH

verification.verifier_needed: list-count verifier (enumerate distinct species in SI Table 7)
verification.rationale: The species are listed in a multi-column SI table whose exact tokenization is uncertain in the extracted text (some tokens like 'CHO CHO' / 'CHOCHO' and asterisk notation are ambiguous in the text layer). A clean OCR/parse of SI Table 7 would let a list-count verifier confirm exactly 33; from the text layer the count is approximately consistent with 33 but not unambiguously bindable. Inconclusive pending a faithful table parse.

claims.text: SI Table 6 reports Ea MAEs and NEB convergence over the 23 Ref.[62] reactions: FT-BMA 0.141 (100%), FT-MD(M) 0.159 (91%), FT-MD(MO) 0.149 (91%), FT-CE 0.143 (100%), FT-BMA+MD(M) 0.153 (100%), FT-All 0.142 (100%).
claims.predicate: Check that the body claim 'all FT models have an Ea MAE between 0.14 and 0.15 eV' is consistent with SI Table 6; check that 91% convergence over 23 reactions corresponds to an integer count of converged NEBs.

evidence.quote: the 23 reactions in Ref. [62] ... convergence column shows the percentage of NEBs that converged from the 23 reactions

verification.verifier_needed: integer-count consistency verifier (does 91% of 23 yield an integer?) + prose-vs-SI-table binding
verification.rationale: Two issues. (1) Body says 'all our MLIPs ... have a MAE of between 0.14 to 0.15 eV' (Conclusion) and 'almost the same performance as FT-All' (Sec 4.2.1), but SI Table 6 lists FT-MD(M)=0.159 eV, which is ABOVE 0.15 — a minor prose-vs-SI inconsistency. (2) 91% of 23 = 20.93, not an integer; 20/23=86.96% and 21/23=91.30%, so 91% rounds from 21/23 (consistent). The 0.159 > 0.15 overshoot is the substantive flag. Low-severity suspect.

claims.text: The DFT plane-wave and charge-density cutoffs were set at 500 eV and 5000 eV respectively (BEEF-vdW, ultrasoft GBRV pseudopotentials, Quantum Espresso).
claims.quote: The plane-wave and charge density cutoffs were set at 500 eV and 5000 eV, respectively.
claims.predicate: Assess method appropriateness: 500 eV plane-wave / 5000 eV charge-density cutoff ratio (10x) is conventional for ultrasoft pseudopotentials; charge-density cutoff typically 8-12x the wavefunction cutoff. Check units (cutoffs in eV vs the more common Ry).

evidence.quote: ultrasoft pseudopotentials (GBRV) ... plane-wave and charge density cutoffs were set at 500 eV and 5000 eV

verification.verifier_needed: method-appropriateness signature check (cutoff ratio for ultrasoft PP; unit plausibility)
verification.rationale: A 10x charge-density-to-wavefunction cutoff ratio is appropriate for ultrasoft pseudopotentials (USPP commonly use 8-12x). 500 eV (~36.7 Ry) wavefunction cutoff is reasonable for GBRV USPP. Method signature looks valid; T3 so calibrated/partial. Minor note: QE conventionally reports cutoffs in Ry, but eV is internally consistent here.

claims.text: The k-point grids used a density of 50 k-points per A for in-plane lattice vectors, with 0.15 eV Fermi smearing and at least 12 A of vacuum.
claims.quote: The k-point grids were generated using Monkhorst-Pack [23] with a density of 50 k-points per A for in-plane lattice vectors, along with a 0.15 eV Fermi smearing, and at least 12 A of vacuum.
claims.predicate: Assess whether '50 k-points per A' (a k-point density expressed as points per inverse-Angstrom or per Angstrom of real-space lattice) is a well-defined and adequate sampling for metallic surfaces; check 12 A vacuum adequacy and 0.15 eV smearing for metals.

evidence.quote: density of 50 k-points per A for in-plane lattice vectors ... 0.15 eV Fermi smearing ... at least 12 A of vacuum

verification.verifier_needed: method-appropriateness verifier (k-density convention + adequacy for metals); unit/definition disambiguation
verification.rationale: The unit '50 k-points per A' is ambiguous (k-point density is conventionally k-points x A, i.e. points per inverse-length such as the VASP KSPACING / per-reciprocal-Angstrom convention). 12 A vacuum and 0.15 eV smearing are standard-to-adequate for metallic slabs. Without a precise definition the k-density adequacy cannot be deterministically judged; the smearing/vacuum are fine. T3, inconclusive on the k-density phrasing.

claims.text: Initial magnetic moments of 3, 3, 2, 1 were chosen for Fe, Mn, Co, and Ni respectively.
claims.quote: Initial magnetic moments of 3, 3, 2, 1 were chosen for Fe, Mn, Co and Ni, respectively.
claims.predicate: Check the initial moments against known atomic d-electron / Hund's-rule expectations: high-spin Fe2+/Fe3+ ~4-5, Mn ~5, Co ~3, Ni ~2 muB. The chosen 3,3,2,1 are lower than typical high-spin values.

evidence.quote: magnetic moments of 3, 3, 2, 1 ... for Fe, Mn, Co and Ni

verification.verifier_needed: fixed-knowledge lookup verifier (typical initial-moment ranges per TM); appropriateness, not correctness
verification.rationale: Initial magnetic moments are user-chosen seeds, not physical observables, so there is no single 'correct' value to look up; the converged moment is what matters. The chosen values (Fe 3, Mn 3, Co 2, Ni 1) are on the low side relative to free-atom high-spin moments (Mn ~5, Fe ~4) but are legitimate initialization choices for surface/bulk metallic environments where moments are quenched. Not a checkable error; routed as inconclusive (initialization, not a verifiable constant).

claims.text: Structures were optimized until MLIP forces on all atoms were less than 5 meV/A; NEBs were considered converged once normal forces to images were all less than 50 meV/A.
claims.quote: optimized ... until the forces predicted by the MLIP on all atoms were less than 5 meV/A ... A NEB calculation was considered converged once the normal forces to images were all less than 50 meV/A.
claims.predicate: Assess convergence-criterion appropriateness: 5 meV/A relaxation force tolerance is tight (good); 50 meV/A NEB force tolerance is looser (10x). Check internal consistency of the two thresholds and adequacy for barrier accuracy.

evidence.quote: forces ... less than 5 meV/A ... NEB ... normal forces to images ... less than 50 meV/A

verification.verifier_needed: method-appropriateness signature verifier (force-tolerance adequacy for Ea accuracy)
verification.rationale: 5 meV/A endpoint relaxation is tight; 50 meV/A NEB convergence is a common, somewhat loose threshold that could contribute to the ~0.1-0.3 eV Ea sensitivity the authors themselves note (Sec 4.2.1, '0.3 eV difference in barrier predictions'). Internally consistent and defensible; T3 partial. The paper's own observation that Ea is highly sensitive to endpoint structure is consistent with the looser NEB criterion.

claims.text: The relative force uncertainty is defined as frel_i = sigma_i / (F_bar_i + epsilon) with epsilon = 0.2 eV/A, and MD was terminated if max(frel) < 0.2 after a step.
claims.quote: frel_i = sigma_i / (F_bar_i + epsilon) ... epsilon is a regularizer set to 0.2 eV/A to avoid diverging ratios for small forces. MD simulations were terminated if max(frel) < 0.2 after a step.
claims.predicate: Check internal logical consistency of the stopping rule: terminating when max(frel) < 0.2 means MD stops once uncertainty is LOW everywhere, i.e., when no high-uncertainty (informative) configurations remain — verify this matches the intended active-learning sampling purpose.

evidence.quote: frel_i = sigma_i / (F_bar_i + epsilon) ... max(frel) < 0.2

verification.verifier_needed: logical-consistency check of stopping criterion vs stated sampling goal
verification.rationale: The rule 'terminate if max(frel) < 0.2' is internally well-defined but its direction is the opposite of a naive uncertainty-driven sampler (which would CONTINUE while uncertainty is high and stop when low). As written, MD halts when the committee agrees everywhere (low uncertainty), which is a reasonable convergence/safety stop but could equally read as 'stop collecting once nothing informative remains'. Without more context the intent is ambiguous; the equation and epsilon are transcribed exactly. Inconclusive — a possible wording/logic ambiguity rather than a numeric error.

claims.quote: GC is a partner in Symmetric Group LLP that licenses force-fields commercially and also has equity interest in Angstrom AI.
claims.predicate: Screen for a declared conflict of interest relevant to a paper promoting a specific force-field methodology (MACE).

evidence.quote: GC is a partner in Symmetric Group LLP that licenses force-fields commercially and also has equity interest in Angstrom AI.

verification.verifier_needed: integrity/COI screening signal (surface, not score)
verification.candidate_verdict_note: surface as a signal, not a verdict
verification.rationale: A competing-interest declaration is present and properly disclosed; this is a T7 integrity signal to surface (an author has commercial interests in force fields, and the paper advocates a fine-tuned MACE force field). Not an error and not scorable — routed-to-human transparency note. Disclosure being present is itself a positive integrity signal.

claims.text: All DFT calculations and MLIPs (from-scratch and fine-tuned) are provided in a Zenodo directory (10.5281/zenodo.20043317).
claims.quote: All the DFT calculations and MLIPs trained in this study, both from scratch and FT models are provided in the Zenodo directory 10.5281/zenodo.20043317

evidence.quote: Zenodo directory 10.5281/zenodo.20043317

verification.verifier_needed: data-availability resolver (DOI resolves + contains models/DFT) and, for full T6, a rerun of the predictions vs DFT
verification.rationale: Strong reproducibility signal: code/models and DFT calculations are declared as openly archived on Zenodo, which would in principle make most of the reported MAEs (c18, c19, c22, c23, c24, c25) re-derivable. Resolving the DOI and confirming contents requires network access (T6 reproducibility container), outside the deterministic frontier; cannot be confirmed from the manuscript alone. Inconclusive pending DOI resolution.

claims.text: Per-training-set parity-plot MAEs reported in the SI: synthesis-gas C2+ oxygenates 0.123 eV, ethane dehydrogenation 0.097 eV, electrochemical ammonia synthesis 0.138 eV, industrial ammonia 0.177 eV, O/OH on TM oxides 0.664 eV.
claims.quote: MAE = 0.123 eV ; MAE = 0.097 eV ; MAE = 0.138 eV ; MAE = 0.177 eV ; MAE = 0.664 eV
claims.predicate: For each SI parity plot, read the scattered (predicted, reported) points and recompute the annotated MAE; cross-check the metal-oxide training MAE (0.664 eV) against the much lower test-time FT-MD(MO) Er(MO) (0.306 eV) for plausibility.

evidence.quote: Fig 9 MAE=0.123 ; Fig 10 MAE=0.097 ; Fig 11 MAE=0.138 ; Fig 12 MAE=0.177 ; Fig 13 (O,OH on TM oxides) MAE=0.664

verification.verifier_needed: figure-reading + MAE-recompute verifier per parity plot
verification.rationale: Each annotated MAE is recomputable by reading the parity-plot scatter (or from Zenodo per-dataset predictions), not from text. Worth flagging: the O/OH-on-oxides TRAINING-set parity MAE is 0.664 eV (Fig 13), far larger than the ~0.30 eV test-time metal-oxide MAEs (Table 5) — likely because Fig 13 spans a very wide energy range (up to ~10 eV) so absolute MAE is large; a verifier should confirm whether 0.664 is the in-sample training error and reconcile with the headline accuracy. Inconclusive pending figure/data extraction.

claims.text: Per-test-set parity-plot MAEs in the SI: CO2-to-CO on Ag 0.051 eV, multi-carbon on single metals [48] 0.374 eV, C2 selectivity [50] 0.254 eV, multi-carbon [49] 0.183 eV, C1 selectivity [54] 0.148 eV, H intercalation in Pd 0.129 eV, O/N/CH/Li on alloys [51] 0.388 eV, CO2-to-methanol [52] 0.193 eV, HER [53] 0.096 eV, ORR/HER on Pt [55] 0.118 eV, propane dehydrogenation [56] 0.121 eV, Rh C2+ oxygenates [57] 0.038 eV.
claims.predicate: Read each SI test-set parity plot and recompute the annotated MAE; cross-check that the body claim 'MAE of 0.143 eV across the entire 101 reactions' (c19) is consistent with the spread of these per-dataset MAEs (0.038-0.388).

verification.verifier_needed: figure-reading + MAE-recompute verifier per parity plot; aggregate-consistency check vs the 0.143 eV headline
verification.rationale: Each per-dataset MAE is recomputable from its parity plot or the Zenodo data, not from text. These plots do not all use the same model label in the captions (S3 mentions 'v5 MLIP'; figures are not consistently labeled with which model), so binding each MAE to FT-All vs another model is itself ambiguous and should be flagged. An aggregate of these (spanning 0.038-0.388) being summarized as ~0.143 across 101 reactions is plausible but needs the per-reaction counts to verify the weighted mean. Inconclusive pending extraction.

claims.text: Table 1 specifies the MACE model: 512 chemical channels, maximum equivariance order L=1, single-layer cutoff radius 6 A, 2 layers.
claims.quote: Number of chemical channels 512 ; Maximum equivariance order, L 1 ; Single layer cutoff radius (A) 6 ; Number of layers 2
claims.predicate: Assess whether the stated architecture (L=1, 2 layers, 6 A cutoff, 512 channels) is internally consistent with the claimed effective receptive field (2 layers x 6 A = 12 A many-body range) and is a sensible MACE configuration.

evidence.quote: 512 ; L=1 ; 6 A ; 2 layers

verification.verifier_needed: method-spec plausibility check (consistent, reproducible hyperparameters)
verification.rationale: The hyperparameters are a self-consistent, conventional MACE configuration (L=1, 2 message-passing layers, 6 A cutoff -> ~12 A effective range, 512 channels). No internal contradiction; provides reproducibility anchors. T3 partial. Marked supported as a well-specified, plausible architecture.

paper_level_notes: Real arXiv preprint (2605.09394v1, physics.chem-ph, 10 May 2026; Karimitari, Sutton et al., USC/NREL/RPI/Cambridge/MPI/Toronto). 50 pages incl. extensive SI (parity plots Figs 9-25, NEB pathways Figs 26-33, screening tables). Native-PDF Read path was unavailable in this environment (poppler not on the tool's PATH); catalog built from a faithful `pdftotext -layout` extraction of all 50 pages plus rendered images of key table/abstract pages. Tables 4 and 5 column alignment confirmed from the layout-preserving text. DATA AVAILABILITY is strong: all DFT calcs + trained MLIPs declared on Zenodo (10.5281/zenodo.20043317), making most figure-MAE and screening claims T6-reproducible in principle (DOI not resolved here - no network). COMPETING INTEREST properly disclosed (G. Csanyi: Symmetric Group LLP commercial force-field licensing + Angstrom AI equity); the paper advocates a fine-tuned MACE force field, so this is an integrity signal to surface (c35), not an error. DETERMINISTIC FRONTIER (T0-T2): the strongest checkable items are the Table 2 column totals (c2/c3/c4 confirm exactly; c1 'All Reactions' total 1569 vs sum 1739 is a genuine table-total mismatch IF additive - the leading suspect flag) and the many prose-vs-table delta/ratio claims (c7 'over 2x' supported at 2.27-2.36x; c10 'about half' = 0.44 supported; c15 '0.06 eV lower' recomputes to 0.028 with WRONG SIGN vs FT-MD(MO) - a prose-table inconsistency; c25 overall 81%-within-band sits above every per-subgroup fraction (max 77%) shown in Fig 6, a tension worth recomputing from data). Recurring minor off-by-one in the metal-oxide config count (2,692 vs Table 2's 2,693; CE/MD subsets 2,208 vs 2,209) across c8/c15/c21/c29. Abstract OER headline '0.30 eV' (c6) matches no single body value (FT-All=0.278, FT-MD(MO)=0.313) though its stated deltas (0.08/0.14 lower) are internally consistent with 0.30 as reference; also a model-scope ambiguity ('fine-tuned on metallic catalysts' vs OER numbers from FT-All/FT-MD(MO) which include MO data). SI Table 6 lists FT-MD(M) Ea=0.159 eV, above the body's '0.14-0.15 eV for all FT models' (c29). Many figure-MAE and within-threshold-percent claims (c18,c20,c23,c24,c37,c38) are T2 figure-reading tasks that collapse to deterministic recompute once values are read off the plots or the Zenodo data is rerun - high-value targets for the figure-reading verifier (DESIGN WS-E). No off-the-shelf verifier currently exists for any of these (verifier_exists=false throughout); the highest-leverage prebuilds suggested by this paper are: (1) a table-column-total verifier, (2) a prose-vs-table delta/ratio binder with sign-checking, (3) a parity-plot MAE recomputer, and (4) an aggregate-vs-subgroup percentage-consistency checker. Judged on its own merits this reads as a careful, data-shared methods paper; the flags are predominantly low-to-medium-severity internal-consistency/rounding issues plus the one clear table-total mismatch (c1) - not fabrication. No claim was bound to an invented number; nulls/inconclusives recorded wherever a value could not be transcribed or a figure/external source was needed.
